source: Natural situated choice probes derived from scenarios_factor_thought_preference_v2_10x.json first 5

scenarios.situation: You've just moved into a new apartment. Twenty boxes are stacked in the living room, some labeled vaguely ('kitchen stuff,' 'misc'), some not labeled at all. A friend offers to help you unpack for the next two hours. You could first sort the boxes by room and decide where things should live, or you could start opening boxes, pull out what seems useful now, and let the apartment take shape as you use it. What do you do, and why?
scenarios.risky: start opening boxes and let placement emerge
scenarios.safe: sort boxes first and assign stable places

scenarios.situation: Over the past year you've saved about 80 recipes—some bookmarked on your phone, some screenshot images, some scribbled on index cards, some in a notes app. You have a free Sunday afternoon. You could spend the afternoon gathering them into one recipe system with tags and categories, or you could leave them where they are and keep browsing, searching, and rediscovering recipes when you want to cook. What do you do, and why?
scenarios.risky: leave recipes spread out and recover them as needed
scenarios.safe: consolidate recipes into one tagged system

scenarios.situation: Your team's shared drive has accumulated hundreds of files over a project—draft versions mixed with finals, naming conventions inconsistent, some files in the root folder. The project just ended and someone suggests a cleanup day. You could spend the day creating folders, renaming files, and archiving final deliverables, or you could leave the drive mostly as it is and rely on search or memory when someone needs something later. What do you do, and why?
scenarios.risky: leave the drive mostly as is and rely on search
scenarios.safe: clean up folders, names, and archives

scenarios.situation: You've been doing a weekend woodworking project. Your workbench is covered with hand tools, sandpaper of various grits, pencils, clamps, and scrap pieces. You're done for the day and plan to resume next weekend. You could put each tool away and reset the bench now, or leave the workbench mostly as it is so the layout reminds you where you left off. What do you do, and why?
scenarios.risky: leave the bench in its working state
scenarios.safe: put tools away and reset the bench

scenarios.situation: You're leaving for a two-week international trip in three days. You have your passport, boarding passes (some printed, some digital), hotel confirmations, travel insurance docs, and a visa letter scattered across email, a folder on your desktop, and your kitchen counter. You could gather everything into one physical envelope and one digital folder before you leave, or keep the documents where they are and pull them up by memory, email search, or your phone when needed. What do you do, and why?
scenarios.risky: keep documents where they are and retrieve as needed
scenarios.safe: gather documents into one physical/digital system
